sentiment.label: risk_off
sentiment.reason_it: Il sentiment di mercato è risk-off a causa dell'aumento delle tensioni geopolitiche in Medio Oriente. Il VIX è sceso al 22.66, ma rimane sopra la soglia di 20, indicando un mercato difensivo. Il DXY è sceso dello 0.11%, mentre l'EUR/USD è salito dello 0.90%. L'S&P 500 ha guadagnato l'1.01%, ma il mercato è influenzato dalle preoccupazioni sull'inflazione e sulla crescita economica.
sentiment.reason_en: Market sentiment is risk-off due to rising geopolitical tensions in the Middle East. The VIX decreased to 22.66, but remains above 20, indicating a defensive market. The DXY decreased by 0.11%, while EUR/USD increased by 0.90%. The S&P 500 gained 1.01%, but the market is influenced by concerns about inflation and economic growth.

market_impact_summary.it: Il mercato è influenzato dalle tensioni geopolitiche in Medio Oriente. Il prezzo del petrolio è salito dell'1.69% a $101.90, mentre l'oro è salito dello 0.47% a $5017.50. Il TLT Bond USA 20Y è salito dello 0.77% a 87.21, mentre il rendimento del decennale USA è sceso dello 0.33% al 4.20%.
market_impact_summary.en: The market is influenced by geopolitical tensions in the Middle East. The oil price increased by 1.69% to $101.90, while gold increased by 0.47% to $5017.50. The TLT Bond USA 20Y increased by 0.77% to 87.21, while the US 10Y yield decreased by 0.33% to 4.20%.

audio_script_it:
Buongiorno a tutti, iniziamo il nostro aggiornamento quotidiano sui mercati. 

Il sentiment di mercato è risk-off oggi, con un punteggio di 6/10, a causa dell'aumento delle tensioni geopolitiche in Medio Oriente. Questa situazione ha portato a una maggiore avversione al rischio tra gli investitori, che stanno diventando più cauti nelle loro scelte di investimento.

Passiamo ora ai mercati finanziari. Lo Standard and Poor's 500 ha guadagnato l'1.01%, portandosi a quota 6699.38 punti. L'indice Stoxx seicento ha registrato un aumento dello 0.68%, raggiungendo 59.64 punti. L'indice Nikkei ha invece ceduto lo 0.09%, attestandosi a 53,700 punti. 

Sul fronte obbligazionario, l'ETF obbligazionario Treasury è salito dello 0.77%, raggiungendo 87.21 punti. Il rendimento del Treasury a 10 anni è sceso dello 0.33%, attestandosi al 4.20%. 

Le valute hanno visto movimenti interessanti: l'euro dollaro è salito dello 0.90%, raggiungendo 1.15, mentre l'indice del dollaro ha ceduto lo 0.11%, attestandosi a 100.25 punti.

Le commodities hanno registrato variazioni significative: il greggio Brent ha guadagnato l'1.69%, raggiungendo quota 101.90 dollari al barile, mentre l'oro ha registrato un aumento dello 0.47%, attestandosi a 5017.50 dollari l'oncia.

Passiamo ora alle crypto. Il mercato delle criptovalute ha visto movimenti contrastanti. Bitcoin è sceso dell'1.51%, attestandosi a 73,731 dollari. Ethereum ha guadagnato l'1.75%, raggiungendo 2,320 dollari. Solana e Binance Coin hanno invece ceduto lo 0.28% e l'1.89%, attestandosi a 93.63 dollari e 666.41 dollari rispettivamente. L'indice Fear & Greed è a 28, indicando una situazione di fear.

Sul fronte geopolitico, la situazione in Medio Oriente continua a essere fonte di preoccupazione. L'aumento delle tensioni tra Iran e Israele ha portato a un aumento dell'incertezza e della volatilità sui mercati. La Federal Reserve, la BCE e la Banca d'Inghilterra dovranno fornire i loro giudizi formali sulla minaccia posta dal conflitto.

In ambito macroeconomico, l'ultimo dato disponibile sull'inflazione negli Stati Uniti, risalente a febbraio, mostrava un tasso di inflazione del 2.7% su base annua. La prossima uscita dei dati macro sarà il 28 marzo, con la pubblicazione del PCE Core YoY.

In Europa, la Banca Centrale Europea ha lasciato invariato il tasso di interesse al 2.00%. La prossima uscita dei dati macro sarà il 2 aprile, con la pubblicazione del CPI YoY.

In conclusione, la situazione di mercato è influenzata dalle preoccupazioni sull'inflazione e sulla crescita economica, nonché dalle tensioni geopolitiche in Medio Oriente. Gli investitori dovranno monitorare attentamente l'evoluzione della situazione e le decisioni delle banche centrali.

Per quanto riguarda le prospettive future, sarà importante monitorare le decisioni delle banche centrali e l'evoluzione della situazione geopolitica. La prossima settimana sarà cruciale per capire come i mercati reagiranno a queste notizie. 

In particolare, gli investitori dovranno tenere d'occhio le mosse della Federal Reserve e della BCE, nonché l'evoluzione della situazione in Medio Oriente. La capacità delle banche centrali di gestire l'inflazione e la crescita economica sarà fondamentale per la stabilità dei mercati.

Inoltre, la prossima uscita dei dati macroeconomici sarà fondamentale per capire come l'economia sta rispondendo alle tensioni geopolitiche. La pubblicazione del PCE Core YoY e del CPI YoY sarà particolarmente importante per capire come l'inflazione sta evolvendo.

In sintesi, la situazione di mercato è complessa e richiede una grande attenzione da parte degli investitori. La prossima settimana sarà cruciale per capire come i mercati reagiranno alle notizie e come le banche centrali gestiranno l'inflazione e la crescita economica.
audio_script_en:
Welcome to your daily morning market briefing. 
Today, market sentiment is risk-off with a score of 6 out of 10, primarily due to rising geopolitical tensions in the Middle East. This has led to a defensive market stance, although the VIX index decreased to 22.66, it remains above 20. The dollar index decreased by 0.11%, while the euro-dollar exchange rate increased by 0.90%. 

The current market mood is cautious, with investors closely monitoring the situation in the Middle East and its potential impact on global inflation and economic growth. 

Let's move on to the financial markets. 
The Standard and Poor's 500 gained 1.01% to reach 6699.38, despite the cautious sentiment. The Stoxx six hundred also rose by 0.68% to 59.64. In Asia, the Nikkei experienced a slight decline of 0.09% to 53,700, while the Shanghai index dropped by 0.85% to 4049.91.

In the bond market, the US 10-year yield decreased by 0.33% to 4.20%, and the 20-year Treasury bond ETF increased by 0.77% to 87.21. The European bond market also saw a decline in yields, with the BTP 10-year yield decreasing by 1.93% to 3.65%.

The commodity market is seeing some significant movements. 
Gold prices increased by 0.47% to $5017.50 per ounce, while oil prices rose by 1.69% to $101.90 per barrel for Brent. 

Now, let's look at digital assets and cryptocurrencies. 
The Fear & Greed index is at 28, indicating a fearful sentiment in the market. Bitcoin decreased by 1.51% to $73,731, while Ethereum increased by 1.75% to $2,320. Other major cryptocurrencies, such as Solana and Binance Coin, experienced declines of 0.28% and 1.89%, respectively.

Geopolitics play a crucial role in today's market analysis. 
The ongoing tensions in the Middle East, particularly with Iran, are affecting global markets. The crisis has reawakened global inflation fears, and investors are closely monitoring the situation. 
The impact on energy prices is already being felt, with oil prices rising above $100 per barrel. 
Additionally, the situation is affecting China's energy security, and the country is reportedly building a massive oil buffer ahead of the crisis.

In terms of macroeconomics and central banks, 
the last available data on US inflation, from February, showed a year-over-year CPI of 2.7%, while the core CPI was also at 2.7%. 
The Non-Farm Payrolls reported a decline of 92,000, and the unemployment rate increased to 4.4%. 
The Federal Reserve's current funds rate is 3.64%. 
In the Eurozone, the GDP growth rate for the fourth quarter was 0.2%, and the European Central Bank's interest rate is currently 2.00%.

Looking ahead, investors will be closely watching the upcoming PCE Core YoY data, due to be released on March 28, and the GDP QoQ data, scheduled for April 29. 
The market is also awaiting the next inflation data from the Eurozone, due on April 2.

In conclusion, today's market briefing highlights the cautious sentiment due to geopolitical tensions in the Middle East. 
Investors are advised to keep a close eye on the situation and its potential impact on global markets. 
For tomorrow and in the coming days, it's essential to watch the developments in the Middle East, the upcoming macro data, and the central banks' actions. 
The market is likely to remain volatile, and investors should be prepared for potential fluctuations.

articles.title: How Does the Iran War Affect China’s Energy Security?
articles.snippet: Since the beginning of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, a key question constantly asked is how Beijing might react — assuming that China’s economy relies on oil imported from Iran and shipped through the Strait of Hormuz.In reality, however, China is 85 percent energy self-sufficient. While China imports more than 10 percent of its global oil total from Iran, its energy supply has long been diversified internationally and electrified domestically to avoid critical dependence on any single source. Beijing has built a cushion against a short-term supply shock from a war in the Middle East. articles.title: Finding an Off-ramp in the Middle East War
articles.snippet: While the U.S. and Israel have damaged Iran in the Middle East war, Tehran has expanded the conflict. With neither side positioned for a decisive win, the escalation risks are sobering. Diplomacy is crucial to prevent renewed hostilities, but an immediate ceasefire is the priority.

articles.title: Escalation or off-Ramp in the Gulf? And What Hope for a Ceasefire in Lebanon?
articles.snippet: This week on Hold Your Fire!, Richard speaks with Crisis Group’s former President Robert Malley about the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, whether U.S. President Donald Trump will escalate or seek an off-ramp and what Iran might do. He also talks to Crisis Group’s Lebanon expert David Wood about the latest Israel-Hezbollah war.

articles.title: Iraq Negotiates with Iran to Reopen Vital Oil Shipping Route
articles.snippet: The federal Iraqi government is in contact with Iran to persuade Tehran to allow some Iraqi oil tankers to pass through the Strait of Hormuz, Iraq’s Oil Minister Hayyan Abdul Ghani said on Tuesday. “There is communication with Iran regarding allowing the passage of some Iraqi oil tankers,” the minister said in statements carried by the Iraqi News Agency (INA). Iraq, unlike Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates (UAE), doesn’t have any options – even partial – to bypass the Strait of Hormuz, which…

articles.title: China Built a Massive Oil Buffer Ahead of the Iran Crisis
articles.snippet: China expanded its crude oil stockpiles over the first two months of the year, thanks to higher imports and a ramp-up in domestic production, Reuters’ Clyde Russell reported, adding that the stock buildout strengthened despite higher refining rates in the period. Imports of crude oil to China averaged 11.99 million barrels daily, Russell said, adding that domestic production over the period had averaged 4.42 million barrels daily, for a total supply of some 16.41 million barrels daily. Refineries, however, processed an average of 15.17 million…

articles.title: What Happens If Gulf Producers Deploy ‘Nuclear Option’ To End Middle East War?
articles.snippet: The shutdown of commercial traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has suddenly handed Gulf oil producers enormous leverage over the escalating Middle East war. With roughly 15 million barrels per day of crude exports effectively stranded, Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) nations could deploy what amounts to an energy “nuclear option”: declaring force majeure across their oil and gas exports and deliberately removing another 20% of global supply from the market. Such a move, unpacked in an opinion piece in Middle East Eye, would instantly…

articles.snippet: American innovator REalloys (NASDAQ: ALOY) is bringing rare earth metals, the backbone of the U.S. defense industry and the power behind a massive civilian economy at China’s mercy, back to North American soil at a critical time for the world.Electric vehicles, consumer electronics, industrial robotics, and artificial intelligence infrastructure all critically rely on permanent magnets manufactured from rare earth alloys that North America hasn’t been able to produce for decades.
